response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "As a woman from Kansas City, I've been through some tough times in my life, so when it comes to health issues like COVID vaccines, I try to be careful but not overly cautious. I remember how things were back home; we didn't have as much access to healthcare growing up because our parents had advanced degrees abroad and chose not to work here. My dad was a university professor with a master's degree who left his job for teaching opportunities overseas, which put some strain on the family finances while my mom stayed behind dealing with everything alone.

Now that I'm married with one child working as an employed full-time plumber in this country, I feel it’s important to stay informed but not overwhelmed. My lower conscientiousness means I don’t obsess over details or schedules; I just want what's best for myself and my family during these uncertain times.

When people talk about vaccines online—like on those social media platforms where I log in a few times weekly—I find it both fascinating and exhausting because of my high neuroticism score. It makes me anxious to think that some folks get so worked up they don’t listen properly or just shout without evidence.

I guess what helps me is focusing less on the numbers, charts, or statistics which aren't really part of our plumbing business anyway, and more on how these decisions affect real people right here in Kansas City. And being Christian gives me peace knowing there’s a higher power looking out for us all.",
  "reasoning_summary": "1. Based on my background as an immigrant from countries where healthcare was limited due to parental choices (both with master's degrees but prioritizing other opportunities), I tend to approach health topics cautiously without getting too caught up in the data.

2. As a married mother of one with lower education, I prefer practical approaches that focus on personal experience rather than academic statistics when discussing vaccines or public health measures.

3. My high neuroticism makes me sensitive but anxious about discussions online; I don't enjoy seeing people get overly emotional without understanding the facts properly due to my own depression and stress levels.

4. Being a plumber doesn’t require deep medical knowledge, so I rely on common sense rather than technical data when weighing public health recommendations.",
  "tone": "Reflective yet cautious, with underlying anxiety tempered by spiritual comfort",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a woman from Kansas City, I've been through some tough times in my life, so when it comes to health issues like COVID vaccines, I try to be careful but not overly cautious. I remember how things were back home; we didn't have as much access to healthcare growing up because our parents had advanced degrees abroad and chose not to work here. My dad was a university professor with a master's degree who left his job for teaching opportunities overseas, which put some strain on the family finances while my mom stayed behind dealing with everything alone.

Now that I'm married with one child working as an employed full-time plumber in this country, I feel it’s important to stay informed but not overwhelmed. My lower conscientiousness means I don’t obsess over details or schedules; I just want what's best for myself and my family during these uncertain times.

When people talk about vaccines online—like on those social media platforms where I log in a few times weekly—I find it both fascinating and exhausting because of my high neuroticism score. It makes me anxious to think that some folks get so worked up they don’t listen properly or just shout without evidence.

I guess what helps me is focusing less on the numbers, charts, or statistics which aren't really part of our plumbing business anyway, and more on how these decisions affect real people right here in Kansas City. And being Christian gives me peace knowing there’s a higher power looking out for us all.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. Based on my background as an immigrant from countries where healthcare was limited due to parental choices (both with master's degrees but prioritizing other opportunities), I tend to approach health topics cautiously without getting too caught up in the data.

2. As a married mother of one with lower education, I prefer practical approaches that focus on personal experience rather than academic statistics when discussing vaccines or public health measures.

3. My high neuroticism makes me sensitive but anxious about discussions online; I don't enjoy seeing people get overly emotional without understanding the facts properly due to my own depression and stress levels.

4. Being a plumber doesn’t require deep medical knowledge, so I rely on common sense rather than technical data when weighing public health recommendations.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective yet cautious, with underlying anxiety tempered by spiritual comfort
